text: Hello everyone. So today we're going to talk about how to do a difficult laparoscopic appendectomy. Now this was a burst appendix with lump formation which we went ahead with an interval appendectomy. I was assisted by Dr. Vipul Bothra. So this was a fifteen year old boy who was initially admitted with pain in abdomen and then was detected to a lump and we deferred his appendectomy.

so the port position remains the same and once you have entered this is the view that we got you know that there was still a significant amount of lump formation with adhesions the momentum was badly stuck and everything was including the cecum and everything was stuck to the lateral abdominal wall so in such cases I think the first thing you need to keep in mind is that you're going to have a little blood loss you know and Why that is significant is not because of the physiology, but because of the fact that the red color of the blood absorbs a lot of light. So immediately as you get that, you're going to have a little drop in your illumination of the field. So if you're not already at a larger illumination and if you think that there is still scope to increase illumination in your light source, please do that because that is something that goes out of our mind when you start operating on such a difficult case and then you realize that, you know, somehow your vision is not good.

The surgeon begins by carefully detaching the omentum from the lateral abdominal wall. Using the Cheetle's maneuver, the cecum is medialized by separating it from the lateral abdominal wall. This allows the surgeon to flip the cecum medially and approach the appendix from the lateral side.

The surgeon approaches the appendix from the lateral side, lifting the cecum to identify the appendix, which is localized in the right paracolic gutter. The peritoneum is carefully stripped off to prevent serosal tears, revealing significant adhesions that are meticulously separated.

The surgeon carefully separates the burst appendix tip, which is buried in the omentum, strand by strand to remove adhesions. The medialized cecum provides ample space for dissection, and the surgeon uses ambidextrous techniques to achieve the correct angle without crossing instruments. Smoke formation and fogging occur as cold CO₂ rushes in, temporarily obscuring the field of vision.

The surgeon carefully holds the appendix while observing the terminal ileum, which is adhered to the appendix near the ileocecal junction. The site of perforation is visible, where the ileum and omentum have adhered, forming a lump. The surgeon strips the serosa from the appendix, taking care to preserve the serosa of the ileum to prevent tears, approaching from the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon carefully strips the peritoneum from the lateral abdominal wall while avoiding damage to the serosa of the cecum and ascending colon during adhesiolysis. Minimal cautery is used to prevent collateral damage, ensuring instruments remain visible and free from unintended contact with surrounding structures. The surgeon reassesses the position of the appendix and ileum, deciding whether further dissection is necessary.

The surgeon performs minimal dissection around the appendix to prevent post-operative adhesions, observing approximately twenty milliliters of bleeding during the laparoscopic procedure. A thorough wash is conducted to clear the surgical field of blood, ensuring adequate illumination and preventing missed collateral damage. The surgeon reassesses the omentum, cecum, and ileum for bleeding, serosal tears, or perforations before proceeding with the appendectomy.

My way of doing it is that I'm going to transfix this. All acute appendicitis, we don't put an endolube, we transfix it. There are two reasons for it. One, a transfixation suture allows me to sleep peacefully at night with no fear of slippage or sloughing off. That's one. Secondly, if I am used to doing this and if I have a perforation close to the base of the appendix and I need to go on to the cecum, I will not convert this into an open surgery for the lack of confidence of suturing. A bowel. So this gives me practice. Maybe one in a hundred patient may have a bad basal perforation where I may need to suture the cecum. In those cases, this practice will help me to make sure that I don't convert it to open just for that one single reason. Secondly, what it does is it improves my intracorporeal suturing technique. Now, appendix is the commonest surgery that you're going to do laparoscopy. whether it's in the beginning of your laparoscopic career or whether it is later in your laparoscopic career. That is the commonest surgery you're going to do. The next commonest after that is usually a hernia or a piloplasty. Now, I don't do hernias, laparoscopic. So for me, next is piloplasty. Piloplasty involves extensive suturing. So how do you bridge this gap? You bridge this gap by transfixing every appendix. Irrespective of whether it is acute, whether it is an elective appendectomy, whether there is a basal perforation or not, I choose to do a transfixation. It improves and gives me real-time practice of my intracorporeal knotting and suturing technique and refines my techniques for more complex procedures subsequently that I am going to do once I am confident of laparoscopic skills.

The surgeon prepares to remove the appendix specimen, which is visibly inflamed and previously perforated, using an endo bag for extraction. Improved illumination is noted after clearing the surgical field of blood.
